narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a model trained on data from thousands of students to predict the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and for you, it estimated a 41.6% chance of failure—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. Your profile includes strong points like a high family income bracket, an LSAT score slightly above average, full-time student status, but also a lower undergraduate GPA of 2.50 compared to the typical 3.21, and notably, your Year 1 and Year 3 school grade deciles both at the 1st decile, far below the averages around the 5th or 6th. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your low Year 3 decile ranking, which stood out as a strong signal of academic struggle relative to peers. On the positive side, your high family income was the top factor pulling toward success, suggesting socioeconomic stability that often helps, closely followed by your solid LSAT score, which demonstrated good test-taking ability. Your low Year 1 decile also contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction, while your undergraduate GPA and full-time status offered minor boosts toward passing. Compared to typical students, your early and later school rankings were in the bottom tier, which weighed heavily against you, even as your LSAT edged out the norm and your background provided some counterbalance. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your grade deciles, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT or GPA improvement resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself, and reapplying after building a stronger academic record. Many have turned similar predictions around with focused effort, and with your resilience, you can too. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
